metadata.description: A Process Resource Entity (resource-service-entities §6) — an automation job as a first-class node in the dependency graph: playbook, workflow, pipeline, or script. Its depends_on edges are PROCESS dependencies and carry the same weight as infrastructure dependencies: while the job is running, nothing it depends on may be taken down. This is what makes orchestrated lifecycle operations (a UPS-triggered graceful shutdown, a rolling upgrade) derivable from true data — the orchestrator host, the switch fabric the job traverses, and the name service it resolves through are the job's real dependencies, so 'orchestrator stops last' and 'connectivity outlives compute' fall out of the reverse-topological sort with no policy special-casing. Vendor engines (Ansible, Tekton, Argo) are providers, never the type. First registry type with entity_type Process; §6.4 obliges entities modified by this job to cite its uuid in their provenance.

context.purpose: Makes an automation job (playbook, pipeline, script) a first-class node in the dependency graph, so what the job needs stays up while it runs.
context.plain_description: A record of a job that automation runs — an Ansible playbook, a CI pipeline, a backup script. It is modeled like infrastructure because it has real dependencies: the host it executes on, the network fabric it traverses, the name service it resolves through. While the job runs, none of those may be taken down, which is how ordered operations like a UPS-triggered graceful shutdown come out of the data instead of hand-written runbooks. The engine that runs the job (Ansible, Tekton, Argo) is a provider detail, not part of the type.
use_when: You need a shutdown or upgrade sequence to know an orchestration job is running and must finish before its executor host stops.
You need to record which automation created or modified a resource, so the resource's provenance can cite the job.
You need a scheduled or event-triggered job (cron, a UPS on-battery event) tracked with a hard execution-time limit.
not_for: The long-running service a job might deploy — that is Software.Service; a service has no scheduled end, a job must declare max_execution_time.
The workload container a job runs in — that is Compute.Container; the job is the process, not the runtime.
works_with: Compute.BareMetalHost — the executor host a running job pins (the orchestrator stops last).
Network.Switch — the fabric the job traverses; connectivity outlives whatever the job operates on.
Network.AddressService — the name resolution the job needs to reach its targets.

process_type.description: Process class (resource-service-entities §6.3). 'other' is the escape hatch — the enum stays closed.

max_execution_time.description: ISO 8601 duration (e.g. PT30M). MANDATORY per ENT-004; enforced by the substrate Lifecycle Constraint Enforcer.

on_max_exceeded.description: Action when max_execution_time is exceeded. Default is profile-governed (ENT-004).

trigger.description: What initiates the job (e.g. a NUT on-battery event, a cron schedule, an operator).

last_result.description: Terminal state of the most recent execution (COMPLETED|FAILED|CANCELLED, §6.2).

relationships.name: executor_host
relationships.description: Executor host(s) — where the job runs. A running job pins its executor: in a reverse-topological lifecycle operation the executor cannot stop until the job completes ('orchestrator stops last', derived, not special-cased).

relationships.name: fabric
relationships.description: Fabric the job traverses to reach its targets — connectivity outlives whatever the job is operating on.

relationships.name: resolver
relationships.description: Name/address resolution the job uses to reach targets (typically soft — inventories can pin IPs).

affected_entities (§6.3) — entities this job creates/modifies/decommissions. Informational; §6.4 makes the provenance obligation flow the OTHER way (affected entity cites the job uuid). NOTE: the declared target is representative — affected entities may be of ANY resource type; this edge is informational and never constrains what a job may touch (the former bare 'Compute' category target was not a registered type).

adopts.role: run-to-completion job vocabulary (activeDeadlineSeconds ≈ max_execution_time, terminal states)
